ace_test_java_patterns.overview: All Java code rules, patterns, and examples for writing ACE v13 JUnit 5 test classes using the com.ibm.integration.test.v1 library (NodeSpy, NodeStub, SpyObjectReference, TestMessageAssembly, TestSetup). Covers: - Node-level unit tests using NodeSpy.evaluate() with recorded .mxml files - Whole-flow integration tests using NodeSpy/NodeStub - Parameterized whole-flow tests using @ParameterizedTest + @CsvSource - The NodeStub API for mocking downstream nodes and external services For recorded .mxml file details, see 5a_recorded_messages.xml. For the step-by-step workflow, see 5_ace_test_project_guide.xml.

java_test_patterns.description: Node-level tests drive a single node in isolation using NodeSpy.evaluate(). Each test verifies what ONE node produces given a recorded input checkpoint. These are the tests generated by ibmint generate tests. All test classes use JUnit 5 (@Test, @AfterEach, @DisplayName).

mandatory_cleanup.description: EVERY test class MUST have an @AfterEach method that calls TestSetup.restoreAllMocks(). This prevents mock state leaking between tests.

critical_rule.title: Only use documented TestMessageAssembly methods — never invent methods
critical_rule.description:
TestMessageAssembly is a final class in com.ibm.integration.test.v1. The ONLY methods available are listed below. DO NOT call any method not on this list — the compiler will reject it with "The method X is undefined for the type TestMessageAssembly".
Known hallucinated methods that do NOT exist: - setMockMessageContent(String, String) ← does not exist - getMessageValue(String) ← does not exist - setMessageBody(String) ← does not exist - getField(String) ← does not exist
There is NO way to construct a TestMessageAssembly from a plain String payload. The ONLY valid ways to populate a TestMessageAssembly are: 1. buildFromRecordedMessageAssembly(InputStream) — from a recorded .mxml file 2. Leave it empty (used internally by NodeSpy/NodeStub)
For node-level unit tests that need specific input, use a recorded .mxml checkpoint as the input — do NOT attempt to construct the message content from a String.

helper_method_load_assembly.description: Always include this private helper for loading .mxml files from the classpath. Use it in node-level tests to load both input and expected assemblies. NOTE: Parameterized whole-flow tests (Part C) use inline loading instead — see below.

pattern.name: node_test_from_recorded_messages
pattern.description: Standard pattern for a node-level test using recorded .mxml files. Replace placeholders: [APP] = application name (e.g. "WeatherAPI_App") [FLOW] = message flow name (e.g. "getCurrentWeather") [NODE] = node label as shown in the flow canvas (e.g. "Prepare Request") [INPUT] = resource path to the input .mxml (the checkpoint BEFORE this node) [EXPECTED] = resource path to the expected output .mxml (the checkpoint FROM this node) [TERMINAL] = output terminal name the node propagates to (e.g. "out", "failure", "out1")

pattern.name: node_test_with_ignore_paths
pattern.description: When the node makes outbound calls (e.g. HTTP Request node) or produces time-varying output, use ignorePath() to exclude fields that legitimately differ from the recording. Common fields to ignore for HTTP Request nodes: /Message/BLOB/BLOB — response body (content changes over time) /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/Date — HTTP date header /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-CachedAt — CDN timestamp /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-RequestId — unique per request /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-Cache — cache hit/miss varies /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/x-weatherapi-qpm-left — rate limit counter /LocalEnvironment/WrittenDestination — contains request timestamps
pattern.code: assertThat(actualOutput, equalsMessage(expectedOutput) .ignorePath("/Message/BLOB/BLOB", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/Date", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-CachedAt", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-RequestId", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-Cache", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/x-weatherapi-qpm-left", false) .ignorePath("/LocalEnvironment/WrittenDestination", true) );
pattern.ignorepath_syntax: ignorePath(path, recursive) path = "/" separated path into the message tree recursive = true → ignore the entire subtree rooted at path recursive = false → ignore only the leaf value at path

pattern.name: subflow_node_reference
pattern.description: For nodes INSIDE a subflow, use .subflowNode() before .node() in the SpyObjectReference.
pattern.code: SpyObjectReference nodeRef = new SpyObjectReference() .application("[APP]") .messageFlow("[FLOW]") .subflowNode("[SubflowNodeLabel]") // label of the subflow instance in the parent flow .node("[NodeInsideSubflow]"); // label of the node inside the subflow

pattern.name: terminal_assert_only
pattern.description: When you only want to check that a node propagated to the correct terminal (without comparing the full message tree), omit the equalsMessage assertion. Useful for quick smoke tests or when the output is non-deterministic.

nodestub_api.description:
NodeStub prevents a node from executing its normal logic and from propagating messages to downstream nodes. It is used alongside NodeSpy for whole-flow integration tests to: - Prevent real outbound calls (HTTP requests, MQ writes) during testing - Isolate specific paths through the flow - Mock external service responses
NodeSpy (used in node-level unit tests) observes a node and lets it run. NodeStub prevents a node from running altogether, acting as a stop point.

pattern.name: whole_flow_with_stubs
pattern.description: Whole-flow test: NodeSpy on the input node injects the recorded message; NodeStubs on all output nodes intercept before they fire; assertions verify messages arrived at the stubs.

nodestub_vs_nodespy.description: Key differences between NodeStub and NodeSpy

row.nodestub: Prevent a node from executing at all
Can feed a mock response back into the flow

row.nodespy: Unit test: test what THIS node produces
row.nodestub: Integration test: mock external services, stop output nodes

parameterized_test_patterns.overview:
Parameterized whole-flow tests allow a single test method to be executed multiple times with different sets of .mxml files — one @CsvSource row per scenario. This is the PREFERRED pattern for manually-written whole-flow tests because: - Adding a new scenario requires only a new @CsvSource row, not a new test method - The column layout (Input | Mock | Expected) makes the intent self-documenting - All scenarios share the same NodeSpy/NodeStub wiring, reducing duplication
These tests are MANUALLY written (not generated by ibmint) and live alongside generated tests in [AppName]_GeneratedTests.
Key difference from Part B: parameterized tests use inline assembly loading (Thread.currentThread().getContextClassLoader().getResourceAsStream(name)) rather than the loadAssembly() helper, because the filename comes in as a parameter.
parameterized_test_patterns.note: Do NOT import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test for parameterized tests — use @ParameterizedTest instead. The two annotations are mutually exclusive on the same method.

core_pattern.name: parameterized_whole_flow
core_pattern.description: The fundamental pattern. Each @CsvSource row drives one complete flow execution. Columns are always ordered: Input, [Mock...], Expected[...]. The method signature mirrors the @CsvSource column order exactly.

variant.name: multiple_output_nodes
variant.description: When the flow fans out to multiple output nodes, add one SpyObjectReference + NodeSpy + setStopAtInputTerminal per output, plus one parameter column per expected output. All outputs are asserted in the same test method.

variant.name: mock_subflow_http_node
variant.description: When the flow makes an outbound HTTP call inside a subflow, use NodeSpy with setStopAtInputTerminal("in") AND setStopAtOutputTerminal("out") on the HTTP Request node to intercept both sides and prevent a live call. Use mock_with_return_value instead when downstream ESQL needs to process the response.

variant.name: mock_with_return_value
variant.description: Use NodeStub.onCall().propagatesMessage() to feed a recorded response back into the flow. Use this when downstream ESQL needs to process the mock response.

variant.name: error_path_catch_terminal
variant.description: For error/catch path tests, drive the flow by propagating from the input node via the "catch" terminal instead of "out". The output assertions target the error output node instead of the normal output nodes.

variant.name: disabled_wip_test
variant.description: Use @Disabled to mark a test whose recorded messages are not yet captured or whose assertions need rework. Always include a reason string. ALWAYS add the import when using @Disabled — it is NOT included in the default imports and the compiler will silently fall back to the fully-qualified name without it, making the code inconsistent. Use the short annotation form @Disabled, never @org.junit.jupiter.api.Disabled.

variant.name: node_stub_bypass
variant.description: When a node must not block the flow but its side-effect must be prevented, use NodeStub.onCall().propagatesInputMessage() to reroute its input to a terminal that harmlessly discards the message.

mistake.problem: @CsvSource column count does not match method parameter count
mistake.symptom: ParameterResolutionException at runtime — test fails before body executes
mistake.solution: Count the columns in the @CsvSource row and ensure the method has exactly that many String parameters

mistake.symptom: Test actually puts messages onto real MQ queues or writes real files during execution
mistake.solution: Call spy.setStopAtInputTerminal("in") on EVERY output node before calling inputSpy.propagate()

mistake.symptom: Test runs only once ignoring all @CsvSource rows
mistake.solution: Replace @Test with @ParameterizedTest. The two annotations are mutually exclusive.

mistake.symptom: Test hangs or throws TestException — flow never starts
mistake.solution: The terminal name must match the actual output terminal in the .msgflow. Common values: "out" for normal path, "catch" for error path.
Always call inputSpy.propagate() FIRST, then retrieve outputs with receivedMessageAssembly()

mistake.problem: Calling a method on TestMessageAssembly that does not exist
mistake.symptom: Java compile error: "The method X is undefined for the type TestMessageAssembly" Common offenders: setMockMessageContent(String, String), getMessageValue(String), setMessageBody(String), getField(String) — NONE of these methods exist.
mistake.solution: Only two operations are valid on TestMessageAssembly: 1. assembly.buildFromRecordedMessageAssembly(InputStream) — populate from a .mxml file 2. assertThat(actual, equalsMessage(expected)) — compare via Hamcrest matcher To retrieve outputs use nodeSpy.propagatedMessageAssembly("terminal", n) or nodeSpy.receivedMessageAssembly("terminal", n). There is NO API to construct a TestMessageAssembly from a raw String payload. If you need specific input for a unit test, use the recorded .mxml checkpoint for that node's input terminal — do not try to build message content from scratch.

aspect.name: Effort to create
aspect.generated: Low — ibmint generate tests produces all test cases automatically
aspect.manual: High — requires understanding the flow, writing Java, choosing assertions

aspect.name: Test coverage
aspect.generated: Node-by-node: each node transition becomes a test case
aspect.manual: Can test complete end-to-end scenarios with multiple assertions

aspect.name: Brittleness
aspect.generated: High — tightly coupled to recorded messages; any flow change may break tests
aspect.manual: Lower — tests are written against the intent of the flow, not exact byte values

aspect.name: Sequential propagation handling
aspect.generated: Poor — assumes parallel propagation, fails for sequential patterns
aspect.manual: Good — can be written to match actual flow execution order

aspect.name: Maintenance
aspect.generated: Difficult — modifying generated tests requires re-understanding recorded data
aspect.manual: Easier — test intent is self-documenting in Java method names and comments

aspect.name: Recommended use
aspect.generated: Migration testing (v12 → v13) and quick regression baseline
aspect.manual: Long-lived test suites, complex flows, pipelines requiring stability

generated_vs_manual_tests.recommendation: Use generated tests to rapidly establish a regression baseline during migration projects. Invest in manual whole-flow integration tests (NodeSpy + NodeStub) for flows that will be actively maintained and evolved. The two approaches complement each other: generated tests catch regressions; manual tests document business requirements.
